title: The artists behind the listening notes.
intro: This index gathers the artists into a simple gallery so the site feels like a complete archive instead of a set of disconnected pages.

artists.name: Kangaroo
artists.genres: City Pop, Jazz Fusion, Disco, Funk
artists.note: A small catalog, but every release feels purposeful and memorable.
artists.lead: A compact, underappreciated band that made a handful of albums and left behind a whole lot of great songs.
artists.notes: They only made four albums before they split, but every one of them feels purposeful and full of personality.

albums.title: Steppin’
albums.description: The record I keep returning to for its consistency and sheer number of strong songs.

albums.title: Sunshine Bright on Me
albums.description: A bright, polished track that feels instantly memorable.

albums.title: A Night in New York
albums.description: One of the songs that makes the band feel a little more cinematic and a little more sly.

artists.reviewText: What I like most is that the band never feels like it is overexplaining itself. The songs are sharp, melodic, and weird in just the right way. Even though they only made a small number of albums, the writing is so consistent that it feels much larger than the catalog suggests.
artists.quote: Steppin’ is the one I would pick first if I had to choose a single album, mostly because it lands so many songs I genuinely want to hear again.

artists.name: Nujabes
artists.genres: Lo-fi Hip Hop, Jazz Hop, Chillhop
artists.note: Calm, reflective, and deeply influential without ever feeling heavy-handed.
artists.lead: An artist whose work feels calm and reflective while still holding a huge amount of emotional weight.
artists.notes: He is often treated as a foundational figure in lo-fi hip hop, and that reputation makes a lot of sense to me.
favoriteSongs: Feather (feat. Cise Starr & Akin from CYNE)

albums.title: Modal Soul
albums.description: The record that feels most complete and most emotionally wide open.

albums.title: Metaphorical Music
albums.description: Often the one I reach for when I want something quietly beautiful.

albums.title: Samurai Champloo
albums.description: A soundtrack that feels like both a companion piece and a whole world of its own.

artists.reviewText: His music has a strange kind of stillness. Even when it is full of motion, the feeling is always deep and reflective. I do not think I have listened to every record he made, but the ones I know already feel like they belong in a very specific emotional space.
artists.quote: Lady Brown, Feather, and Luv(sic.) Pt. 2 are the songs I keep returning to when I want something that feels both elegant and human.

artists.name: Casiopea
artists.note: A band that made fusion jazz feel immediate, fun, and wonderfully tight.
artists.lead: A fusion-jazz band that made the genre feel more immediate and more fun than I expected.
artists.notes: The older records are the ones that stick with me the most, even if some of the newer material is also very strong.
favoriteSongs: Domino Line (Live at Chuo Kaikan Hall, Tokyo, Feb. 1982)

albums.title: Casiopea
albums.description: A record so consistent that I can put it on and lose track of time.

albums.title: Mint Jams
albums.description: Live energy, but still very focused and smart.

albums.title: Photographs
albums.description: A record that feels polished, spacious, and wonderfully playable.

artists.reviewTitle: Why Casiopea keeps pulling me back
artists.reviewText: They made me rethink how much I enjoy fusion jazz. The music is technically impressive, but it never feels like a display of technique alone. It is also one of those bands where the sound is weirdly easy to love even if you are not deeply into the genre.
artists.quote: Midnight Rendezvous, Time Limit, and Swallow are the tracks that absolutely make the case for them.

artists.name: T-Square
artists.note: Bright, melodic, and polished without feeling overworked.
artists.lead: A jazz fusion band with a long history and a very strong sense of melodic structure.
artists.notes: I have not listened to them in huge depth, but what I know already feels very memorable.

albums.title: Adventures
albums.description: The most influential and most immediately rewarding record in the set.

albums.title: Takarajima
albums.description: A bright, melodic piece that still feels very alive.

albums.title: Sunnyside Cruise
albums.description: A lighter, more playful track that still feels polished and memorable.

artists.reviewText: What I like most is how much of the band's personality comes through even in a short listen. Their records feel polished but not cold, and the songs often have a kind of bright momentum that makes them easy to revisit.
artists.quote: Adventures is the one that feels most substantial, not only because of the songs but because of the place it holds in the wider history of the genre.

artists.name: Masayoshi Takanaka
artists.note: Warm, glossy, and full of personality from the first listen.
artists.lead: A guitarist and composer whose records feel cut from the same bright, polished, impossible-to-ignore thread.
artists.notes: I came to him more recently than some of the others, but the records I have heard already feel very memorable.

albums.title: Seychelles
albums.description: The one that feels the most cohesive and the most immediately lovable.

albums.title: All of Me
albums.description: A record that feels warm, polished, and rich in atmosphere.

albums.title: An Insatiable High
albums.description: A more vivid and slightly more restless record, but very rewarding.

artists.reviewText: The guitar work is one part of it, but what keeps me interested is how much personality is packed into each record. There is a real sense of style here, and it is the kind of style that makes me want to hear more instead of less.
artists.quote: Tokyo Reggie, Sexy Dance, and Palm Street are the songs that make the whole thing feel especially alive.

artists.name: Himiko Kikuchi
artists.note: A later discovery that has already become one of the most rewarding listens in this set.
artists.lead: A later discovery for me, but one that has already become one of the most rewarding listens in this whole set.
artists.notes: I only really knew her through Flying Beagle at first, but Sevilla Breeze has become just as important to me.

albums.title: Sevilla Breeze
albums.description: The record that now feels like the clearest statement of what makes her work so good.

albums.title: Flying Beagle
albums.description: The album that introduced me to her and still holds a lot of charm.

artists.reviewTitle: Why Himiko Kikuchi matters to me
artists.reviewText: Her work is one of those discoveries that grows on me the more I hear it. It is not just that the songs are good; it is that they feel distinct from the other artists I am listening to right now, and that makes them easier to remember.
artists.quote: Look Your Back!, Sevilla Breeze, and Flying Beagle are the songs that made me want to keep going.

artists.name: Spectrum
artists.genres: Jazz Fusion, Jazz-Rock, City Pop, Big Band/Brass
artists.note: A bright, big-band-leaning fusion act with a few songs that I keep coming back to.
artists.lead: I have not listened to their discography in depth, but the songs I know already give off a huge, polished, ensemble-driven energy.
artists.notes: Their sound is one of the most immediately recognizable in this set because of the big-band feel and the way the brass lines carry the hooks.

albums.title: Spectrum
albums.description: A strong introduction to the band’s big, melodic side.

albums.title: Optical Sunrise / Spectrum 2
albums.description: The album with the highest concentration of songs I already love.

albums.title: Spectrum Super Remix 1991
albums.description: A very imaginative remix record that makes the originals feel new again.

artists.reviewText: What I like most is the scale of the sound. It feels huge without losing the melodic clarity that makes the songs stick.
artists.quote: Even a small taste of Spectrum makes you want to hear more of the larger, brass-forward world it creates.

artists.name: Castlevania Sound Team / Michiru Yamane
artists.genres: Video Game Soundtrack, Gothic Rock, Orchestral
artists.note: A soundtrack catalog that stays consistently excellent and emotionally huge.
artists.lead: The Castlevania music is one of the biggest reasons I keep coming back to the series, and Michiru Yamane is a huge part of that legacy.
artists.notes: The PS2-era soundtracks are especially important to me because they feel both grand and very playable on repeat.

albums.title: Castlevania: Curse of Darkness
albums.description: The soundtrack I keep returning to for its emotional range and replay value.

albums.title: Castlevania: Symphony of the Night
albums.description: A classic with some of the most memorable tracks in the whole series.

albums.title: Castlevania: Aria of Sorrow
albums.description: A darker, more intimate set that still feels very alive.

favoriteSongs: Don’t Wait Until Night / Heart of Fire
artists.extraNote: This entry is especially personal because Castlevania is probably my favorite game series of all time.
artists.reviewTitle: Why Castlevania’s music matters to me
artists.reviewText: The music is one of the reasons the series feels so timeless. It is dramatic, melodic, and often surprisingly intimate at the same time.
artists.quote: Even when I only want a few tracks, the whole catalogue feels like a world I want to keep exploring.

artists.name: Falcom Sound Team jdk
artists.genres: Video Game Soundtrack, Rock, Orchestral, Electronic
artists.note: A newer discovery with a huge range of moods, styles, and memorable themes.
artists.lead: I only recently started paying attention to Falcom, but the music already feels rich, varied, and very easy to love.
artists.notes: The selection feels broad enough that it can shift from grand orchestral pieces to more hard-edged electronic and rock-driven tracks without losing identity.

albums.title: Adol Christin - Ys 35th Anniversary
albums.description: The most rewarding compilation for me so far, especially because it feels curated and comprehensive.

albums.title: Ys IX - Super Ultimate
albums.description: A soundtrack that feels confident, dramatic, and very polished.

albums.title: Ys VIII - Lacrimosa of DANA
albums.description: A strong example of how wide the style range can get without feeling scattershot.

favoriteSongs: At the End of a Disaster
artists.reviewText: The biggest thing that stands out is the range. There is so much confidence in the writing that even a short listen makes me want to explore more.
artists.quote: It feels like the kind of soundtrack catalog that rewards curiosity more than any single album can contain.

artists.name: Ridge Racer Series
artists.genres: Video Game Soundtrack, Techno, Trance, Drum and Bass, Acid Jazz, Breakbeat
artists.note: A series whose soundtrack has stayed consistently excellent from the start.
artists.lead: This is one of those series soundtracks that feels almost unfairly strong, even though I have never really played the games themselves.
artists.notes: The music is one of the clearest examples of a soundtrack carrying the identity of an entire series across multiple eras.
artists.favoriteAlbum: R4 - Ridge Racer Type 4 Original Soundtrack

albums.title: R4 - Ridge Racer Type 4 Original Soundtrack
albums.description: The soundtrack I would reach for first because it feels complete and wonderfully consistent.

albums.title: Ridge Racer Original Soundtrack
albums.description: A classic showcase of the series’ bright, kinetic style.

albums.title: Ridge Racer 2 Original Soundtrack
albums.description: A very strong follow-up with plenty of momentum and personality.

artists.reviewTitle: Why Ridge Racer stays with me
artists.reviewText: The soundtrack is so strong that it almost feels like the series’ true identity. It balances energy with enough texture to stay interesting across many listens.
artists.quote: Even without playing the games, the music makes the whole franchise feel vivid and memorable.

artists.name: Uyama Hiroto
artists.genres: Jazz Fusion, Acoustic Jazz, Ambient, Nu Jazz
artists.note: A warm, lyrical, mostly instrumental artist whose music feels both calm and deeply rewarding.
artists.lead: I found him through suggestions for artists similar to Nujabes, and what stands out is how much more instrumental and acoustic his work feels while still carrying the same reflective charm.
artists.notes: His music feels more grounded in acoustic and instrumental textures than in beat-driven production, and that makes it feel very distinctive to me.

albums.title: A Son of the Sun
albums.description: The album I would pick first because it is so full of beautiful acoustic songs and a very strong sense of atmosphere.

albums.title: Freedom of the Son
albums.description: A record that feels open, melodic, and deeply reflective.

albums.title: 2nd Collection
albums.description: A strong showcase of his lyrical, understated style and the warmth of his composing.

artists.reviewText: What I like most is how calm and human his music feels. It is not trying to overpower you; it simply gives you a lot of beautiful, carefully shaped ideas to settle into.
artists.quote: A Son of the Sun is the one I keep coming back to because it feels so complete and so full of genuinely good acoustic songs.
